MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS
Off Calendar Nov-18-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Defendant Catherine Colely's motion to quash is granted.

"When a defendant challenges the court's personal jurisdiction on the ground of improper service of process the burden is on the plaintiff to prove. . . the facts requisite to an effective service." (Summers v. McClanahan (2006) 140 Cal.App.4th 403, 413 (quotation marks omitted).) Plaintiff contends that it properly substitute served Colely at her "dwelling house [or] usual place of abode" within the meaning of California Code of Civil Procedure sec. 415.20(b). Defendant presents persuasive evidence that the address of service, a beach house in North Carolina, is in fact not Colely's usual place of abode. Rather, the premises is Colely's parents' beach house. (Melaney Coley Decl., pars. 1-4.) Substitute service at the residence of a relative is not substantial compliance with the substitute service statute. (See Zirbes v. Stratton (1986) 187 Cal.App.3d 1407, 1416 [substitute service at defendant's parent's residence was defective].) Therefore, plaintiff failed to effectuate proper service.

In opposition, plaintiff relies on hearsay to maintain its burden. The security guard at the North Carolina residence allegedly confirmed that Colely "owns property on the island." (Geonetta Decl., Ex. 7 [proof of service/declaration of diligence].) The Court sustains Colely's hearsay objection to this evidence. Moreover, even if Colely "owns property on the island," there is no showing that the island property is her "usual place of abode" or whether Colely owns a residence different from her parents' premises.

Plaintiff's citation to Bein v Bechtel-Jochim Group Inc. (1992) 6 Cal.App.4th 1387 is distinguishable. In that case, where a gate guard was substitute served, there was no dispute regarding the defendant's residence. (See id. at 1393 ["[Defendants] authorized the guard to control access to them and their residence."].)

Any request to serve Defendant by publication must be directed to the Presiding Judge in Dept. 206. (See SF Local R

Order is signed.
